title: First-Order Signature
gloss: A signature is the formal vocabulary available before any axioms are chosen. It says which symbols may occur and how many input places each symbol has.

title: Arity
gloss: Arity is bookkeeping for grammatical well-formedness. It determines whether a symbol has been supplied with the correct number of arguments.

title: Language
gloss: The signature supplies the subject-specific vocabulary. The language adds the logical grammar needed to form expressions from that vocabulary.

title: Constant Symbol
gloss: A constant symbol is syntactically rigid: unlike a variable, it does not vary with an assignment. In the arithmetic signature, the symbol $0$ is a piece of formal syntax; under the standard interpreta

title: Function Symbol
gloss: A function symbol is operation-forming syntax. In the arithmetic signature, $S$ is unary, while $+$ and $\cdot$ are binary. Under an interpretation, each becomes an actual operation on the chosen doma

title: Relation Symbol
gloss: A relation symbol is a statement-forming symbol once it is applied to the right number of terms. In the arithmetic signature, $+$ is a binary function symbol because it returns a number, while $<$ is

title: Axiom
gloss: An axiom is not part of the vocabulary itself. It is an assertion made using the vocabulary of a language. The signature determines which symbols may appear; the axiom states something with those symb

title: Axiom System
gloss: An axiom system records the assertions chosen for a mathematical setting. Two axiom systems may use the same language while adopting different assertions. The shared language fixes the vocabulary; the

title: Assumption
gloss: An axiom is adopted as part of the formal background of a system. An assumption is local to an argument: it may be introduced to prove a conditional, to reason within a case, or to derive a contradict

title: Theory
gloss: A theory is not merely the list of axioms. It is the closure of those axioms under derivability: every statement that follows from the axioms belongs to the theory. The axioms start the system; the th

title: Theorem
gloss: Within an axiom system, a theorem is not an additional starting assumption. It is a statement reached from the adopted axioms by reasoning. Axioms are given; theorems are obtained.

title: Consequence
gloss: Consequence is the relation that connects a theory to what it implies. If the axioms generate a theory, then consequences are the statements carried along by those axioms and by the statements already

title: Deductive Closure
gloss: Deductive closure is the operation that turns an axiom system into the full theory it generates. Starting with the axioms, close under derivability; the result is the theory determined by those starti

title: Interpretation
gloss: An interpretation turns formal vocabulary into mathematical content. It tells what each constant, function symbol, and relation symbol means in a particular setting.

title: $ $-Structure
gloss: A structure is the environment in which formal symbols receive meaning. The domain supplies the objects, and the interpretation explains how the symbols refer to objects, operations, and relations on

title: Model
gloss: A model is a structure that makes the theory true. It is not just an interpretation of the vocabulary; it is an interpretation in which the adopted assertions and their consequences hold.

title: Satisfaction
gloss: Satisfaction is the semantic test for a statement. Once the symbols have been interpreted in a structure, the statement can be checked as true or false in that setting.

title: Countermodel
gloss: A countermodel shows that the theory does not force the statement. It keeps the axioms true while making the proposed statement false.

title: Contradiction
gloss: A contradiction marks a breakdown in compatibility. It identifies an assertion that cannot be made true in the interpretation under consideration.

title: Consistent Axiom System
gloss: Consistency says that the adopted assertions fit together. A consistent axiom system avoids forcing an impossible situation.

title: Inconsistent Axiom System
gloss: Inconsistency means that the adopted assertions cannot all be maintained coherently. The system asks for incompatible requirements at once.

title: Model Existence Criterion (Informal)
gloss: This is an informal criterion in this section, not a formal theorem. The idea is that a model provides a setting in which all the axioms hold together, so the axioms have at least one coherent realiza

title: Relative Consistency
gloss: Relative consistency does not establish consistency from nothing. It says that the target theory introduces no new contradiction unless the base theory was already inconsistent. In practice, a relativ

title: Universal Instantiation
gloss: A universally quantified statement may be specialized to any admissible instance.

title: Quantifier Distribution
gloss: When a hypothesis does not depend on the quantified variable, a universal conclusion may be drawn uniformly under that hypothesis.

title: Variable
gloss: A variable is a basic syntactic placeholder. It can occur inside terms and formulas, and later sections explain how quantifiers bind occurrences of variables.

title: Constant Symbol
gloss: A constant symbol is a simple name-forming symbol in the grammar. It supplies a base case for terms alongside variables.

title: Function Symbol
gloss: A function symbol is an operation-forming symbol in the grammar. It is not a term by itself; it becomes part of a term only after it is supplied the required number of input terms.

title: Predicate Symbol
gloss: Predicate symbols are the atomic-formula-forming vocabulary of the language. They are syntactic symbols, not project metalanguage predicates.

title: Term
gloss: Terms are the noun-like expressions of first-order syntax. They provide the inputs to predicate symbols but do not themselves make assertions.

title: Atomic Formula
gloss: Atomic formulas are the base cases of formula formation. They are the first strings in the grammar that count as formulas.

title: Formula
gloss: The language supplies the symbols and formation rules; the formula notion is the syntactic category governed by those rules.

title: Well-Formed Formula
gloss: Well-formed formulas are exactly the grammatical formula strings of the language. Later semantic and proof-theoretic sections apply only to these strings.

title: Molecular Formula
gloss: Molecular formulas are the compound formulas of first-order syntax. They are formed after the atomic stage by applying a connective or quantifier.

gloss: [Free for Substitution] A term $t$ is $x$ in $ $ if no free occurrence of $x$ in $ $ lies within the scope of a quantifier $ y$ or $ y$ where $y$ is a variable occurring in $t$. Equivalently, $t$ is f

title: Alpha-Equivalence
gloss: [Alpha-Equivalence] Formulas that differ only by the names of bound variables are logically equivalent. This is called (or ): \[ x\, \; \; y\, \]

title: First-Order Language
gloss: A first-order language is still syntax. It says which expressions can be formed, but it does not assign objects, functions, or relations to the non-logical symbols.

title: Structure
gloss: A structure is the minimal semantic object needed in Volume I. It gives meaning to the non-logical symbols of a language. Systematic topics such as substructures, expansions, reducts, and elementary c

title: Variable Assignment
gloss: Structures interpret non-logical symbols; assignments interpret variables. Open formulas require assignments, while sentences eventually do not depend on the particular assignment.

title: Modified Assignment
gloss: Modified assignments are the semantic mechanism behind quantifiers. To test a quantified formula, the assignment is varied at the bound variable while all other variable values remain fixed.

title: Term Evaluation
gloss: Term evaluation is the semantic counterpart of term formation. It assigns a domain element to each grammatical term once a structure and assignment are fixed.

title: Satisfaction
gloss: Satisfaction is the bridge from grammar to semantic use. It is recursive because formulas are recursive. The assignment is essential exactly where free variables can affect the value of the formula.

title: Truth in a Structure
gloss: Sentences have no free variables. Therefore the particular assignment does not carry mathematical information once the formula is a sentence; the notation \(\mathcal M\models\varphi\) suppresses it.

title: Validity
gloss: Validity is truth by logic alone. It does not depend on a special structure, special domain, or special interpretation of the non-logical symbols.

title: Satisfiability
gloss: Satisfiability asks whether a formula can be made true somewhere. This is the semantic counterpart of consistency only in the minimal Volume I sense; deeper proof-theoretic distinctions are deferred.

title: Model of a Sentence
gloss: A model is not an extra kind of object. It is a structure viewed relative to a sentence that it makes true.

title: Model of a Set of Sentences
gloss: The notation \(\mathcal M\models T\) abbreviates a universal check over the sentences in \(T\). No additional theory of model classes is developed here.

title: Theory
gloss: This is intentionally modest. Complete theories, elementary classes, categoricity, saturation, types, diagrams, and elementary extensions are Volume VIII topics. A theory may be called satisfiable in

title: Logical Consequence
gloss: Logical consequence is semantic entailment. The premises in \(T\) leave no room, across structures, for \(\varphi\) to fail.

title: Logical Equivalence
gloss: Logical equivalence is semantic interchangeability at the sentence level. It records sameness of truth across all structures in the relevant language.

title: Open Formula
gloss: Open formulas are syntactic predicates in the lightest Volume I sense. They do not by themselves name a set-theoretic relation until a structure is fixed.

title: Definable Relation
gloss: A relation is a set-theoretic subset of \(D^n\). A definable relation is one obtained from a formula after a structure supplies semantic meaning.

title: Substitution Lemma for Terms
gloss: The lemma says that syntactically substituting a term before evaluation gives the same domain element as first evaluating the substituting term and then updating the assignment.

title: Substitution Lemma for Formulas
gloss: This lemma connects the syntactic substitution operation from the syntax section with the semantic modified-assignment operation from the semantics section. The free-for condition prevents variable ca

title: Universal Formula
gloss: A universal formula asserts that its scope holds for every value assigned to the bound variable.

title: Existential Formula
gloss: An existential formula asserts that its scope holds for at least one value of the bound variable.

title: Bounded Quantifier
gloss: The restricted universal uses implication, while the restricted existential uses conjunction. This preserves the usual behavior on empty sets.

title: Sentence
gloss: Sentences have truth values in structures without reference to a particular assignment.

title: Open Formula
gloss: Open formulas may become true or false only after a structure and assignment are fixed.

title: Quantifier Scope
gloss: Scope determines which variable occurrences a quantifier controls. Scope errors are the main source of incorrect translations from English into predicate logic.

title: Negation of Bounded Quantifiers
gloss: The domain restriction is preserved under negation. What changes is the quantifier and the inner assertion.

title: Quantifier Negation Laws
gloss: Negating a quantified statement flips the quantifier and pushes the negation into the scope.

title: Double Negation and Quantifiers
gloss: Double negation does not change the quantifier form once the two negations are cancelled.

title: Same-Type Quantifier Commutation
gloss: Two universal choices can be checked in either order, and two existential witnesses can be selected in either order.

title: Mixed Quantifiers Do Not Commute
gloss: The first form permits the witness \(y\) to depend on \(x\). The second form requires one uniform witness \(y\) that works for every \(x\).

title: Valid Quantifier Distribution
gloss: Universal quantification distributes over conjunction because both conjuncts must hold for every value. Existential quantification distributes over disjunction because a witness for either disjunct wi

title: Invalid Quantifier Distribution
gloss: The invalid laws fail because witnesses or counterexamples may vary between the two subformulas.

title: Vacuous Quantification
gloss: A quantifier that binds no occurrence has no semantic effect.

title: Renaming Bound Variables
gloss: Bound variable names are placeholders. Renaming them changes notation, not meaning, provided no free variable becomes captured.

title: Quantifier Prefix
gloss: A prefix records all quantifier choices before the quantifier-free part of a prenex formula is read.

title: Matrix
gloss: The matrix is the part of a prenex formula where the propositional connectives and atomic formulas remain after all quantifiers have been pulled forward.

title: Prenex Formula
gloss: Prenex formulas isolate quantifier order from the quantifier-free matrix.

title: Prenex Normal Form
gloss: Prenex normal form changes the placement of quantifiers but not the meaning of the formula.

title: Existence of Prenex Normal Form
gloss: Every formula can be converted into an equivalent one with all quantifiers at the front and a quantifier-free matrix at the end.

title: Stronger Formula
gloss: The stronger sentence has fewer possible structures in which it is true. It rules out more cases.

title: Weaker Formula
gloss: The weaker sentence permits more structures. It follows from the stronger sentence but carries less information.

title: Quantifier Order
gloss: Quantifier order controls dependency. In \(\forall x\,\exists y\), the witness \(y\) may depend on \(x\). In \(\exists y\,\forall x\), the same \(y\) must work for all \(x\).

title: Universal Implies Existential
gloss: If every domain element satisfies \(\varphi\), then at least one does because first-order structures have nonempty domains.

title: Existential Does Not Imply Universal
gloss: One witness does not make a universal claim true.

title: Uniform Witness Implies Dependent Witness
gloss: A single witness \(y\) that works for every \(x\) can be reused as the witness required for each separate \(x\).

title: Dependent Witness Need Not Be Uniform
gloss: The dependent-witness statement allows \(y\) to vary with \(x\). The uniform statement requires one \(y\) to handle all \(x\) at once.

title: Universal Instantiation — UI
gloss: [Universal Instantiation — UI] From a universally quantified statement, infer any instance obtained by substituting a term for the bound variable: \[ x\, \; \; . \]

title: Universal Generalization — UG
gloss: [Universal Generalization — UG] From a formula, infer a universally quantified statement: \[ \; \; x\, . \]

title: Existential Instantiation — EI
gloss: [Existential Instantiation — EI] From an existentially quantified statement, infer an instance with a fresh constant (witness): \[ x\, \; \; . \]

title: Existential Generalization — EG
gloss: [Existential Generalization — EG] From a formula containing a term, infer an existential statement: \[ \; \; x\, . \]

title: Equality Introduction — Reflexivity
gloss: [Equality Introduction — Reflexivity] For any term $t$, one may infer $t = t$.

title: Equality Elimination — Substitution of Equals
gloss: [Equality Elimination — Substitution of Equals] From $t_1 = t_2$ and $ $, one may infer $ $.

title: Symmetry of Equality
gloss: [Symmetry of Equality] From $t_1 = t_2$, one may infer $t_2 = t_1$.

title: Transitivity of Equality
gloss: [Transitivity of Equality] From $t_1 = t_2$ and $t_2 = t_3$, one may infer $t_1 = t_3$.

title: Term Substitution under Equality
gloss: [Term Substitution under Equality] If $t_1 = t_2$, then for any function symbol $f$, $f( ,t_1, ) = f( ,t_2, )$.

title: Predicate Substitution under Equality
gloss: [Predicate Substitution under Equality] If $t_1 = t_2$ and $P$ is an $n$-ary predicate symbol, then $P( ,t_1, ) \; \; P( ,t_2, )$.

title: Soundness
gloss: [Soundness] A proof system is if every provable formula is valid: \[ . \]

title: Completeness
gloss: [Completeness] A proof system is if every valid formula is provable: \[ . \]

title: Soundness of First-Order Logic
gloss: [Soundness of First-Order Logic] The standard inference rules for first-order logic (UI, UG, EI, EG, and the propositional rules) are sound: they preserve truth in all structures. If $ $, then $ $.

title: Translation Key
gloss: A translation key prevents hidden changes of meaning. The same symbol must keep the same reading throughout one exercise unless the key is explicitly replaced.

title: Domain of Discourse
gloss: The domain decides what the quantifiers are allowed to talk about. If the domain is people, then an exam must either be named by a separate sort-like predicate or represented by a different translatio

title: Predicate and Relation Symbols in a Translation
gloss: Object-language predicate symbols such as \(P,Q,R\) are symbols inside the formal language. Metalinguistic predicate readings such as \(\operatorname{Term}(t)\) or \(\operatorname{AtomicFormula}(\varp

title: Constants and Variables in a Translation
gloss: Constants do not range. Variables do range when bound by quantifiers. Confusing the two usually leaves a formula with the wrong dependency pattern.

title: Universal Translation Pattern
gloss: The antecedent \(A(x)\) restricts attention to the relevant objects. Objects outside \(A\) do not have to satisfy \(B\).

title: Existential Translation Pattern
gloss: The witness must satisfy both restrictions at once. Existence is not merely a conditional promise about what would happen if an \(A\) existed.

title: Conditional Translation Pattern
gloss: Conditionals are especially common inside universal translations. The conditional separates the restricting condition from the asserted consequence.

title: Negation Translation Pattern
gloss: The negation of ``all'' is ``some not.'' The negation of ``some'' is ``none.'' For restricted universal claims, negating the implication produces a witness that satisfies the restriction and fails the

title: Scope Ambiguity
gloss: Scope is not cosmetic. Changing the outer quantifier can change whether a single witness works for everyone or whether each object may have its own witness.

title: Universal Affirmative Form
gloss: The \(A\)-form says that every object satisfying the subject predicate also satisfies the predicate predicate. It does not by itself say that anything satisfies the subject predicate.

title: Universal Negative Form
gloss: The \(E\)-form says that anything satisfying \(A\) fails to satisfy \(B\). It is still universal, so it can be true when no object satisfies \(A\).

title: Particular Affirmative Form
gloss: The \(I\)-form requires a witness satisfying both predicates. It carries existential force.

title: Particular Negative Form
gloss: The \(O\)-form requires a witness satisfying the subject predicate and failing the predicate predicate.

title: A-O Contradiction
gloss: The \(O\)-form is exactly the counterexample to the \(A\)-form: an object that is \(A\) but not \(B\).

title: E-I Contradiction
gloss: The \(I\)-form is exactly the counterexample to the \(E\)-form: an object that is both \(A\) and \(B\).

title: Contrariety Requires Existential Import
gloss: With an \(A\)-object available, the two universal claims force both \(B\) and \(\neg B\) of that object. Without such an object, both universals may be vacuously true. The traditional square of opposi

title: Propositional Weakening
gloss: If $P$ is available, then it remains available under any additional assumption $Q$.

title: Implication Distribution
gloss: Implications may be composed under a common hypothesis.

title: Classical Contraposition
gloss: This is the classical contrapositive principle. It is the axiom schema that marks the background logic as classical rather than intuitionistic.

title: Truth Value
gloss: Truth values are the semantic targets of propositional formulas. Syntax determines which strings are formulas; semantics later assigns elements of \(\mathbb{B}\) to formulas.

title: Propositional Language
gloss: The language supplies symbols and grammar. It does not yet assign truth values, truth conditions, or logical consequence.

title: Propositional Variable
gloss: A propositional variable has no internal syntactic structure in propositional logic. It is a basic symbol from which more complex formulas are formed.

title: Logical Connective
gloss: Arity records how many formulas a connective uses to form a new formula. The truth behavior of these connectives belongs to semantics, not to the syntactic definition.

title: Well-Formed Formula
gloss: Well-formed formulas are exactly the strings to which the semantic and proof-theoretic machinery of propositional logic applies.

title: Closure Under Formation
gloss: Closure says that every legal use of a formation rule produces another well-formed formula. It is the forward direction of the recursive grammar.

title: Minimality of Well-Formed Formulas
gloss: Minimality is the formal content of the clause ``nothing else is a wff.'' It says that \(\WFF\) is contained in every set of strings that contains the propositional variables and is closed under the f

title: Atomic Formula
gloss: Atomic formulas are the base cases of the recursive grammar. They contain no connective structure.

title: Molecular Formula
gloss: Molecular formulas are exactly the formulas obtained by applying negation or a binary connective at least once.

title: Main Connective
gloss: The main connective identifies the outermost syntactic operation. It is the first connective used when analyzing a formula from the outside inward.

title: Structural Induction for Propositional Formulas
gloss: To prove a statement for all formulas, it suffices to prove it for propositional variables and show it is preserved by each formula-forming operation.

title: Structural Recursion for Propositional Formulas
gloss: Structural recursion justifies recursive definitions such as variables of a formula, subformulas, depth, connective count, syntax tree, and truth evaluation.

title: Constructor Disjointness for Propositional Formulas
gloss: A formula cannot be parsed as two different outer formation types.

title: Constructor Injectivity for Propositional Formulas
gloss: If two formulas are built by the same outer constructor and are equal as strings, then their immediate constituents are equal.

title: Unique Decomposition for Propositional Formulas
gloss: Unique decomposition makes recursive definitions and induction on formulas unambiguous.

title: Parse Tree
gloss: The parse tree records the same structure described by unique decomposition. It turns the recursive construction of a formula into a visible tree.

title: Unique Syntax Tree for Propositional Formulas
gloss: A formula has exactly one tree representation of its recursive construction. This is the tree form of unique decomposition.

title: Variable Set of a Formula
gloss: The variable set records exactly which propositional variables occur in a formula.

title: Subformula
gloss: Subformulas are the formulas that occur at nodes of the parse tree.

title: Proper Subformula
gloss: Proper subformulas are the strict syntactic parts of a formula. They exclude the whole formula itself.

title: Formula Depth
gloss: Formula depth measures the height of the syntax tree. Some authors call this the rank, complexity, or formation depth of the formula.

title: Connective Count
gloss: Formula depth measures the height of the syntax tree. Connective count measures the number of connective nodes in the syntax tree.

title: Proper Subformula Depth
gloss: Every descent into a proper subformula lowers formula depth. This justifies induction on formula depth and recursive proofs over subformulas.

title: Finiteness of Variables in a Formula
gloss: Every formula depends on only finitely many propositional variables, even though the language may contain infinitely many variables.

title: Finiteness of Subformulas
gloss: Every formula has only finitely many syntactic parts.

title: Truth Assignment
gloss: A truth assignment gives semantic values to the atomic formulas. The recursive truth conditions for the connectives then determine the truth value of every well-formed formula.

title: Domain of a Truth Assignment
gloss: A truth assignment is global: it assigns truth values to all propositional variables in the language, even when a particular formula uses only finitely many of them.

title: Extension of a Truth Assignment to Formulas
gloss: The original assignment \(v\) gives truth values only to propositional variables. The extension \(\widehat v\) evaluates every formula by following the formula's syntactic construction.

title: Unique Extension of a Truth Assignment
gloss: This theorem guarantees that semantic evaluation is well-defined. Once the truth values of the propositional variables are fixed, every well-formed formula has exactly one truth value.

title: Satisfaction of a Formula
gloss: The notation \(v\models\varphi\) means that the formula \(\varphi\) is true under the truth assignment \(v\).

title: Satisfaction of a Set of Formulas
gloss: Satisfaction of a set means simultaneous satisfaction of every formula in the set.

title: Truth Table
gloss: A truth table is finite because every formula contains only finitely many variables. It records the complete truth behavior of a formula.

title: Tautology
gloss: A tautology is true under every possible assignment of truth values to propositional variables.

title: Contradiction
gloss: A contradiction has no true row in its truth table.

title: Satisfiable Formula
gloss: A satisfiable formula has at least one true row in its truth table.

title: Contingency
gloss: A contingency has at least one true row and at least one false row in its truth table.

title: Logical Consequence
gloss: Logical consequence means truth preservation from premises to conclusion. There is no truth assignment under which all premises are true and the conclusion is false.

title: Logical Equivalence
gloss: Logically equivalent formulas have identical truth-table columns. They are interchangeable for semantic purposes.

title: Coincidence Lemma for Propositional Logic
gloss: The truth value of a formula depends only on the variables actually occurring in that formula.

title: Finite Truth-Table Theorem
gloss: Truth tables are finite because every formula contains only finitely many propositional variables.

title: Boolean Function
gloss: Boolean functions are the abstract truth-functions that formulas may represent.

title: Truth Function Induced by a Formula
gloss: The induced truth function packages the truth table of a formula as a Boolean function.

title: Counting Boolean Functions
gloss: The domain \(\mathbb{B}^n\) has \(2^n\) elements. A Boolean function chooses one of two truth values for each input row.

title: Propositional Equivalence Law
gloss: Equivalence laws are the algebraic rewrite rules of propositional logic. They allow formulas to be transformed without changing truth conditions.

title: Logical Equivalence is an Equivalence Relation
gloss: Logical equivalence behaves like equality at the semantic level.

title: Basic Boolean Equivalence Laws
gloss: These are the basic algebraic laws for conjunction, disjunction, and negation.

title: Identity and Domination Laws
gloss: Tautologies act like true constants; contradictions act like false constants.

title: De Morgan Laws for Propositional Logic
gloss: Negation swaps conjunction with disjunction when pushed inward.

title: Distributive Laws for Propositional Logic
gloss: Conjunction distributes over disjunction, and disjunction distributes over conjunction. These laws are the main algebraic engine behind CNF and DNF conversion.

title: Absorption Laws
gloss: Once \(\varphi\) is already required, adding \(\varphi\lor\psi\) contributes no additional constraint. Dually, once \(\varphi\) is already allowed, adding \(\varphi\land\psi\) contributes no additiona

title: Conditional Equivalence Laws
gloss: A conditional fails exactly when the antecedent is true and the consequent is false.

title: Biconditional Equivalence Laws
gloss: A biconditional says that two formulas have the same truth value: both true or both false.

title: Formula Context
gloss: A formula context is a syntactic environment into which a formula can be inserted. Contexts make precise the idea of replacing a subformula inside a larger formula.

title: Replacement of Logical Equivalents
gloss: Logical equivalence is substitutive: equivalent formulas may be replaced inside larger formulas without changing truth behavior.

title: Literal
gloss: Literals are the indivisible signed atoms used in normal forms. They remember which propositional variable is being tested and whether it appears positively or negatively.

title: Positive Literal
gloss: A positive literal asserts the propositional variable directly. It carries no leading negation.

title: Negative Literal
gloss: A negative literal denies exactly one propositional variable. In normal forms, negation is pushed down until it appears only at this literal level.

title: Complementary Literals
gloss: Complementary literals are the local contradiction pattern inside clauses and terms. The pair \(P,\neg P\) is tied to one variable, not merely to two formulas with opposite truth values under a partic

title: Clause
gloss: A clause records a finite list of literal-level alternatives. It is satisfied when at least one of its literals is true.

title: Term
gloss: A term records a finite list of literal-level requirements. It is satisfied when all of its literals are true.

title: Empty Clause
gloss: A nonempty clause is true when at least one listed literal is true. With no literals listed, there is no witness to make the disjunction true, so the empty clause is the false boundary case for clause

title: Empty Conjunction
gloss: A nonempty term is true when every listed literal is true. With no literals listed, there is no requirement to violate, so the empty conjunction is the true boundary case for terms.

title: Negation Normal Form
gloss: Negation normal form is the first cleanup stage for propositional formulas. It does not force a formula to be a disjunction of terms or a conjunction of clauses; it only restricts the available connec

title: NNF Conversion Procedure
gloss: The procedure is a syntax-directed rewrite process. Each rewrite step is backed by a logical equivalence law, so the output formula has the same truth conditions as the input formula.

title: Existence of Negation Normal Form
gloss: Every propositional formula can be rewritten so that negation appears only at the variable level. This makes negation normal form a semantic normalizing step: it changes shape while preserving logical

title: Elementary Conjunction
gloss: Elementary conjunctions are the conjunction terms used as the building blocks of disjunctive normal form. They record simultaneous literal requirements.

title: Minterm
gloss: A minterm relative to \(P_1,\ldots,P_n\) specifies one complete truth-value assignment on those variables: choosing \(P_i\) requires \(P_i\) to be true, and choosing \(\neg P_i\) requires \(P_i\) to b

title: Disjunctive Normal Form
gloss: DNF is a syntactic form, but it is used semantically: once a formula is replaced by a \hyperref[def:logical-equivalence-propositional-logic]{logically equivalent} DNF formula, questions about \hyperre

title: Existence of Disjunctive Normal Form
gloss: One first converts \(\varphi\) to negation normal form, then repeatedly applies distributive laws to move conjunctions below disjunctions. Replacement of logical equivalents preserves equivalence at e

title: DNF Satisfiability Criterion
gloss: A DNF formula is true exactly when at least one of its conjunction terms is true. A conjunction term can be made true exactly when it does not require both a variable and its negation.

title: Elementary Disjunction
gloss: Elementary disjunctions are the clause-level building blocks used in conjunctive normal form. They record finite literal alternatives.

title: Maxterm
gloss: A maxterm relative to \(P_1,\ldots,P_n\) specifies one literal-level alternative for every variable in the list. It is false under exactly the assignment that makes each listed literal false.

title: Conjunctive Normal Form
gloss: CNF is a syntactic form used to expose clause-by-clause constraints. Replacing a formula by a \hyperref[def:logical-equivalence-propositional-logic]{logically equivalent} CNF formula turns global vali

title: Existence of Conjunctive Normal Form
gloss: One first converts \(\varphi\) to negation normal form, then repeatedly applies distributive laws to move disjunctions below conjunctions. Replacement of logical equivalents preserves equivalence thro

title: Truth-Table Row Conjunction
gloss: The row conjunction names exactly one truth-table row. It is true on that row and false on every row that disagrees with it on at least one listed variable.

title: Truth-Table Row Clause
gloss: The row clause excludes exactly one truth-table row. It is false on row \(r\) and true on every row that disagrees with \(r\) on at least one listed variable.

title: Canonical Disjunctive Normal Form
gloss: Canonical DNF records exactly the truth-table rows on which the formula is true, using one row conjunction for each true row.

title: Canonical Conjunctive Normal Form
gloss: Canonical CNF records exactly the truth-table rows on which the formula is false, using one row clause for each false row.

title: Canonical Disjunctive Normal Form Theorem
gloss: Canonical DNF rebuilds the formula from exactly the truth-table rows where the formula is true. Each row conjunction recognizes one true row, and the final disjunction accepts any of those rows.

title: Canonical Conjunctive Normal Form Theorem
gloss: Canonical CNF rebuilds the formula by excluding exactly the truth-table rows where the formula is false. Each row clause rejects one false row, and the final conjunction requires all false rows to be

title: Semantic Classification by Normal Forms
gloss: The truth-table method classifies \(\varphi\) by evaluating every row directly. The normal-form method first rewrites \(\varphi\) into an equivalent DNF or CNF formula and then checks the finite terms

title: Representation of a Boolean Function
gloss: A formula represents a Boolean function when the formula computes the same truth value as the function for every input row. This turns a semantic table of values into a syntactic object inside the pro

title: DNF Representation of Boolean Functions
gloss: A truth table specifies which input rows make the Boolean function true. For each true row, form the corresponding conjunction of literals; the disjunction of those row conjunctions represents the ent

title: Connective Basis
gloss: A connective basis controls which connectives are primitive, not which truth functions are expressible. Different bases may have the same expressive power.

title: Definable Connective
gloss: Definability separates notation from expressive power. A connective can be omitted from the primitive syntax without losing its use, provided it is recoverable as a defined connective.

title: Functionally Complete Connective Set
gloss: Functional completeness is the central expressive-power property for connective sets. It says that the basis can express all possible truth-functional behavior, not merely the connectives originally l

title: Expressively Equivalent Connective Sets
gloss: Expressive equivalence lets a proof replace one primitive vocabulary with another. This is why a language can be simplified syntactically without changing which truth functions can be expressed.

gloss: Canonical disjunctive normal form uses only literals, conjunctions, and disjunctions. Since literals use variables and negation, the normal-form theorem shows that \(\{\neg,\land,\lor\}\) can express

title: Connective Reduction
gloss: Reduction shows that some primitive connectives are optional. If a connective is definable from the remaining basis, then formulas using that connective can be translated away.

title: NAND Connective
gloss: NAND is read as "not both." It is false only when both inputs are true. This single connective can reproduce negation, conjunction, and disjunction.

title: NAND-Only Formula
gloss: A NAND-only formula is syntactically restricted but expressively powerful. The restriction is severe at the level of primitive symbols, but not at the level of truth functions.

title: NAND is Functionally Complete
gloss: NAND can define negation and conjunction: \[ \neg\varphi\equiv\varphi\uparrow\varphi, \qquad \varphi\land\psi\equiv(\varphi\uparrow\psi)\uparrow(\varphi\uparrow\psi). \] Once negation and conjunction

title: NOR Connective
gloss: NOR is read as "neither." It is true exactly when both inputs are false. Like NAND, this single connective can reproduce the standard functionally complete basis.

title: NOR-Only Formula
gloss: A NOR-only formula uses a single primitive connective. Its expressive power comes from the ability of NOR to define negation and disjunction.

title: NOR is Functionally Complete
gloss: NOR can define negation and disjunction: \[ \neg\varphi\equiv\varphi\downarrow\varphi, \qquad \varphi\lor\psi\equiv(\varphi\downarrow\psi)\downarrow(\varphi\downarrow\psi). \] Once negation and disjun

title: Incomplete Connective Set
gloss: Incompleteness is usually proved by finding an invariant preserved by every formula built from the basis. If some Boolean function fails that invariant, the basis cannot express every Boolean function

title: Monotone Boolean Function
gloss: The connectives \(\land\) and \(\lor\) are monotone. Negation is not monotone, because changing \(P\) from false to true changes \(\neg P\) from true to false.

title: Conjunction and Disjunction are not Functionally Complete
gloss: Every formula built using only \(\land\) and \(\lor\) represents a monotone Boolean function. Since negation is not monotone, no formula using only \(\land\) and \(\lor\) can represent negation. Hence

title: Minimal Complete Basis
gloss: Minimality is relative to deletion of connectives from the basis. It does not mean that formulas using the basis are short or easy to read.

title: NAND and NOR are Minimal Complete Bases
gloss: A one-connective complete basis is automatically minimal once the empty basis is not functionally complete. NAND and NOR are therefore minimal complete bases because each is complete by itself.

title: Argument Form
gloss: An argument form records a pattern of inference independently of the particular subject matter. Its validity depends on truth preservation, not on whether the individual formulas happen to be true in

title: Valid Argument Form
gloss: Validity is a preservation property. A valid argument form may have false premises in a particular instance, but it cannot have true premises and a false conclusion under the same truth assignment.

title: Counterexample Assignment
gloss: A counterexample assignment is the semantic witness to invalidity. Finding one settles the question: the argument form is not truth-preserving.

title: Truth-Table Validity Test for Argument Forms
gloss: The truth-table test is finite because only the variables appearing in the premises and conclusion matter. A failed row is a counterexample assignment; the absence of such rows proves semantic validit

title: Modus Ponens is Valid
gloss: Modus ponens is the basic rule for using a conditional whose antecedent has already been established.

title: Modus Tollens is Valid
gloss: Modus tollens is the semantic form of denying an antecedent by denying the conditional's consequent.

title: Hypothetical Syllogism is Valid
gloss: Hypothetical syllogism justifies chaining conditional statements.

title: Disjunctive Syllogism is Valid
gloss: Disjunctive syllogism eliminates one disjunct after its negation is known.

title: Addition is Valid
gloss: Addition introduces a disjunction from a known true disjunct.

title: Simplification is Valid
gloss: Simplification extracts a component from a true conjunction.

title: Conjunction Introduction is Valid
gloss: Conjunction introduction combines independently established formulas into one conjunction.

title: Constructive Dilemma is Valid
gloss: Constructive dilemma is a proof-by-cases pattern with conditionals attached to both cases.

title: Destructive Dilemma is Valid
gloss: Destructive dilemma combines disjunction with modus tollens-style reasoning.

title: Affirming the Consequent is Invalid
gloss: A conditional can be true because both antecedent and consequent are true, but also because the antecedent is false. Therefore truth of the consequent does not force truth of the antecedent.

title: Denying the Antecedent is Invalid
gloss: A conditional does not say that the antecedent is the only way for the consequent to be true. The antecedent can fail while the consequent still holds.

title: Rule of Replacement
gloss: Rules of replacement differ from rules of inference. Inference rules move from premises to conclusions; replacement rules rewrite formulas without changing truth behavior.

title: Replacement Rules Preserve Logical Equivalence
gloss: Replacement rules justify algebraic manipulation of formulas. Each local rewrite preserves truth under every truth assignment, so the entire finite rewrite chain also preserves truth under every truth

title: Informal Proof Pattern
gloss: Informal proof patterns are not a substitute for proof. They are templates for arranging valid moves so that a proof has a clear logical shape.

title: Tautological Implication Gives a Valid Argument Form
gloss: This theorem converts a tautology check into an argument-validity check. It is one reason truth tables can validate named inference rules.

title: Satisfiable Set of Propositional Formulas
gloss: Satisfiability of a set is a compatibility condition. The formulas in \(\Gamma\) may impose many requirements, but they are satisfiable only if all of those requirements can be met by the same truth a

title: Unsatisfiable Set of Propositional Formulas
gloss: Unsatisfiability is global failure of joint truth. It does not say that every formula in \(\Gamma\) is false; it says that no single truth assignment can make them all true at once.

title: Finite Subset of a Set of Propositional Formulas
gloss: A finite subset is a finite test case cut out of a larger set of formulas.

title: Finitely Satisfiable Set of Propositional Formulas
gloss: Finite satisfiability says that no finite test has found a contradiction. It does not name a single truth assignment for all of \(\Gamma\); it only says that each finite test can be passed.

title: Unsatisfiability Witness
gloss: An unsatisfiability witness is a subcollection that already fails to be jointly satisfiable.

title: Finite Unsatisfiability Witness
gloss: A finite unsatisfiability witness is a finite obstruction. It says that the failure of \(\Gamma\) is already visible before one inspects all formulas in \(\Gamma\).

title: Finite Unsatisfiability Witness
gloss: An unsatisfiable finite subset is decisive. Adding more formulas cannot restore a truth assignment that already fails on the finite subset.

title: Finite Witness Contrapositive
gloss: This is the easy direction connecting full satisfiability to finite satisfiability: one truth assignment that works for all formulas also works for any finite selection.

title: Propositional Compactness
gloss: Propositional compactness turns infinitely many finite checks into one global truth assignment. Equivalently, every propositional inconsistency has a finite witness.

gloss: Injectivity survives composition: equality after $g\circ f$ can be pulled back first through $g$ and then through $f$.

title: Composition of Surjections Is Surjective
gloss: [Composition of Surjections Is Surjective] $ (f) (g) (g f)$.

title: Composition of Bijections Is Bijective
gloss: A bijection is an injective and surjective function. Since both of those properties are preserved by composition, bijectivity is preserved too.

title: Inverse of a Bijection Is a Bijection
gloss: [Inverse of a Bijection Is a Bijection] $ (f) (f^ -1 )$.

title: Preimage Distributes over Union and Intersection

title: Pointwise Sum of Functions
gloss: The sum is computed output by output; at each input, add the two function values at that same input.

title: Pointwise Difference of Functions
gloss: The difference subtracts the output of $g$ from the output of $f$ at each input separately.

title: Pointwise Product of Functions
gloss: The product is not composition: it multiplies the two real outputs at the same input.

title: Pointwise Scalar Multiple of a Function
gloss: Scalar multiplication stretches every output of the function by the same real scalar.

title: Pointwise Absolute Value of a Function
gloss: The absolute value operation applies the ordinary real absolute value to each output.

title: Pointwise Maximum of Two Functions
gloss: At each input, the new function keeps the larger of the two output values.

title: Pointwise Quotient of Functions
gloss: The quotient is defined pointwise only when the denominator function never vanishes on the domain.

title: Bounded Above Function
gloss: Bounded above means all output values lie below one common real ceiling.

title: Bounded Below Function
gloss: Bounded below means all output values lie above one common real floor.

title: Bounded Function

title: Supremum of a Function on a Set
gloss: The supremum is the least real ceiling for the output set $f(A)$; it need not be an output value of $f$.

title: Infimum of a Function on a Set
gloss: The infimum is the greatest real floor for the output set $f(A)$; it need not be an output value of $f$.

title: Maximum Point of a Function
gloss: A maximum point is an input where the largest output value is attained.

title: Minimum Point of a Function
gloss: A minimum point is an input where the smallest output value is attained.

title: Increasing Function
gloss: As the input moves to the right in $A$, the output is not allowed to go down.

title: Strictly Increasing Function
gloss: Strict increase requires every genuine move to the right in the domain to produce a genuine increase in output.

title: Decreasing Function
gloss: As the input moves to the right in $A$, the output is not allowed to go up.

title: Strictly Decreasing Function
gloss: Strict decrease requires every genuine move to the right in the domain to produce a genuine decrease in output.

title: Monotone Function
gloss: A monotone function moves consistently in one direction: nondecreasing or nonincreasing.

title: Constant Function
gloss: A constant function assigns the same real value to every input in its domain.

title: $ $-Neighbourhood
gloss: The $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood is the open interval of radius $\varepsilon$ centered at $x$.

title: Deleted $ $-Neighbourhood
gloss: The deleted neighbourhood removes the base point. It is the punctured input region used in limit definitions.

title: Cluster Point

title: Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points

title: Adherent Point
gloss: An adherent point can be witnessed by points of $X$ arbitrarily close to $x$, including possibly $x$ itself.

title: Isolated Point
gloss: An isolated point belongs to the set but has a neighbourhood containing no other points of the set.

title: Interior Point
gloss: An interior point has a full open neighbourhood contained in the set.

title: Boundary Point
gloss: A boundary point sees both the set and its complement in every neighbourhood.

title: Interior
gloss: The interior collects exactly the points around which the set contains a whole neighbourhood.

title: Boundary
gloss: The boundary records the points where every neighbourhood touches both inside and outside of the set.

title: Closure
gloss: The closure adds all adherent points of $X$, so it fills in the limiting points that can be reached by points of $X$.

title: Elementary Properties of Closures
gloss: Closure preserves inclusions and finite unions, but intersection can lose limit points that are approached separately by the two sets.

title: Closed Subset of $ $
gloss: $E$ is closed iff it contains its own boundary. Examples: $[a,b]$ closed; $(a,b)$ not closed; $\mathbb{R}$ and $\emptyset$ both closed; $\mathbb{Q}$ not closed.

title: Closed iff Sequentially Closed
gloss: A closed set cannot be escaped by convergent sequences. Every adherent point has a witness sequence; closedness forces the limit into the set.

title: Every Point of an Interval Is a Cluster Point
gloss: This justifies intervals as natural domains for function limits. $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ requires $\operatorname{ClusterPoint}(c, \operatorname{dom}(f))$; on any interval this is free.

title: Bounded Subset of $ $
gloss: A bounded subset of $\mathbb{R}$ fits inside some symmetric interval $[-M,M]$.

title: True Near a Point
gloss: $\lim_{x\to x_0}f(x)=L$ states: for every $\varepsilon>0$, the property $|f(x)-L|<\varepsilon$ is true near $x_0$.

title: Addition Preserves Strict Inequality
gloss: [Addition Preserves Strict Inequality] For all $a, b, c $, \[ a < b \; \; a + c < b + c. \]

title: Addition Preserves Nonstrict Inequality

title: Addition of Strict Inequalities
gloss: [Addition of Strict Inequalities] For all $a, b, c, d $, \[ a < b \; \; c < d \; \; a + c < b + d. \]

title: Addition of Nonstrict Inequalities
gloss: [Addition of Nonstrict Inequalities] For all $a, b, c, d $, \[ a b \; \; c d \; \; a + c b + d. \]

title: Mixed Addition of Inequalities
gloss: [Mixed Addition of Inequalities] For all $a, b, c, d $, \[ a b \; \; c < d \; \; a + c < b + d. \]

title: Multiplication by a Positive Scalar Preserves Strict Inequality
gloss: [Multiplication by a Positive Scalar Preserves Strict Inequality] For all $a, b, c $, \[ a < b \; \; 0 < c \; \; ac < bc. \]

title: Multiplication by a Negative Scalar Reverses Strict Inequality
gloss: [Multiplication by a Negative Scalar Reverses Strict Inequality] For all $a, b, c $, \[ a < b \; \; c < 0 \; \; ac > bc. \]

title: Multiplication by a Positive Scalar Preserves Nonstrict Inequality
gloss: [Multiplication by a Positive Scalar Preserves Nonstrict Inequality] For all $a, b, c $, \[ a b \; \; 0 < c \; \; ac bc. \]

title: Squeeze

title: Transitivity of Strict Inequality
gloss: [Transitivity of Strict Inequality] For all $a, b, c $, \[ a < b \; \; b < c \; \; a < c. \]

title: Mixed Transitivity
gloss: [Mixed Transitivity] For all $a, b, c $, \[ a b \; \; b < c \; \; a < c. \]

gloss: [Square Is Strictly Monotone on Nonneg Reals] For all $a, b $, \[ 0 a < b \; \; a^2 < b^2. \]

title: Square Root Is Monotone on Nonneg Reals
gloss: [Square Root Is Monotone on Nonneg Reals] For all $a, b $, \[ 0 a b \; \; . \]

title: Bernoulli's Inequality
gloss: Bernoulli's inequality is proved by induction and is a key estimate in analysis: it provides a linear lower bound for exponential growth and is used to establish the divergence of the harmonic series,

title: Absolute Value
gloss: The absolute value measures magnitude independent of sign. On the real line it equals the distance from $a$ to $0$: $\lvert a \rvert = d(a, 0)$ under the standard metric. The two-branch definition par

gloss: [Absolute Value Zero Iff Zero] For all $a $, \[ a = 0 a = 0. \]

title: Absolute Value Equals Self or Negation
gloss: [Absolute Value Equals Self or Negation] For all $a $, either $ a = a$ or $ a = -a$.

title: Absolute Value Is Symmetric
gloss: [Absolute Value Is Symmetric] For all $a $, \[ -a = a . \]

title: Absolute Value of a Product
gloss: [Absolute Value of a Product] For all $a, b $, \[ ab = a \, b . \]

gloss: [Strict Modulus Inequality] For all $a $ and $r > 0$, \[ a < r -r < a < r. \]

title: Triangle Inequality

title: Sequence
gloss: A sequence is not a set of terms but a function: the index carries independent weight. Two sequences $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ in $X$ are equal precisely when $x_n = y_n$ for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$, so or

title: Constant Sequence
gloss: A constant sequence has no genuine dependence on the index. The indexing is still part of the object, but every index is assigned the same real value.

title: Null Sequence
gloss: A null sequence is a sequence whose tail is eventually contained in every symmetric neighborhood of zero. The first finitely many terms are irrelevant; only the eventual behavior matters.

title: Constant Sequence Convergence
gloss: A constant sequence is already equal to its proposed limit at every index. Every positive tolerance is therefore satisfied by any tail of the sequence.

title: Zero Sequence is Null
gloss: The zero sequence satisfies every null-sequence tolerance immediately because the distance from each term to zero is exactly zero.

title: Constant Null Sequence
gloss: A sequence that never changes can approach zero only when its fixed value is already zero. Conversely, the fixed value zero gives the zero sequence.

title: Ultimately Constant Sequence
gloss: An ultimately constant sequence may vary on an initial finite segment, but its tail becomes fixed. The concept isolates eventual behavior from finite initial behavior.

title: Difference from the Limit is Null
gloss: Subtracting the proposed limit translates the convergence problem to the special case of convergence to zero. Null sequences are therefore the local normal form for sequence convergence.

title: Ultimately Constant Sequence Convergence
gloss: Once the tail is equal to a fixed value, all sufficiently late terms are already exactly at the proposed limit.

title: Constant Implies Ultimately Constant
gloss: A constant sequence has already stabilized from the first index, so it is automatically stabilized on some tail.

title: Ultimately Zero Sequence is Null
gloss: If the tail is identically zero, then every sufficiently late term lies inside every positive tolerance around zero.

title: Ultimately Constant Null Sequence
gloss: Only the eventual value controls whether an ultimately constant sequence is null. A finite initial segment cannot prevent or create null behavior.

title: Tail Equality Preserves Convergence
gloss: Convergence is a tail property. Changing finitely many initial terms cannot change whether a sequence converges to a specified real number.

title: Sequence Bounded Above
gloss: A bounded-above sequence has a finite ceiling. Failure means no real number serves as a ceiling for all terms.

title: Sequence Bounded Below
gloss: A bounded-below sequence has a finite floor. Failure means no real number serves as a floor for all terms.

title: Bounded Sequence
gloss: A bounded sequence is trapped in a finite symmetric band around zero. This is equivalent to having both a finite ceiling and a finite floor.

title: Eventually Bounded Above Tail Formulation
gloss: One-sided boundedness is unchanged by ignoring finitely many initial terms, because finitely many exceptional values can be absorbed into a larger upper bound.

title: Eventually Bounded Below Tail Formulation
gloss: One-sided lower boundedness is unchanged by ignoring finitely many initial terms, because finitely many exceptional values can be absorbed into a lower bound.

title: Eventually Bounded Tail Formulation
gloss: Boundedness is a tail property up to finitely many exceptions. A finite initial segment can be enclosed by increasing the bound.

title: Bounded Sequence If and Only If Bounded Above and Below
gloss: An absolute-value bound gives a symmetric interval around zero. Having both a floor and a ceiling gives an interval that may not be symmetric, but it can be enlarged to a symmetric one.

title: Absolute Bound Gives Upper and Lower Bounds
gloss: An absolute-value inequality simultaneously controls the positive and negative directions. The upper barrier is $K$, and the lower barrier is $-K$.

title: Upper and Lower Bounds Give an Absolute Bound
gloss: Two one-sided barriers can always be replaced by one symmetric barrier around zero. Any positive $K$ at least as large as both $|m|$ and $|M|$ works.

title: Convergence of a Sequence

title: Convergence --- Neighborhood Formulation
gloss: The neighborhood formulation reframes convergence as a set-containment condition rather than an inequality condition. The assertion $x_n \in V_\varepsilon(L)$ is identical to $|x_n - L| < \varepsilon$

title: Equivalence of Convergence Formulations
gloss: The four conditions are the same geometric fact written in four notational registers. Condition (a) is the informal assertion. Condition (b) is the canonical $\varepsilon$-$K$ definition: the absolute

title: Uniqueness of Limits

title: Limit Preserves Eventual Order
gloss: An inequality that holds on a tail survives passage to the limit, but only as a weak inequality. Strict inequalities on the terms may collapse at the limit, as in $0<x_n$ with $x_n\to 0$.

title: Strict Limit Separation Gives Eventual Order

title: Eventual Strict Comparison Preserves Weak Limit Order

title: Constant Comparison for Sequence Limits
gloss: This is the constant version of the comparison theorem. A fixed real number $B$ may be treated as the constant sequence with value $B$, so each conclusion is a direct application of eventual order pre

title: Constant Squeeze Theorem
gloss: This is the degenerate squeeze theorem: the lower and upper barriers are the same constant sequence. It says that eventual equality to a real number is enough for convergence to that real number.

title: Sequence Squeeze Theorem
gloss: The middle sequence is trapped between two tails that approach the same number. Once both barriers lie inside an $\varepsilon$-neighborhood of $L$, the trapped sequence must also lie inside that neigh

title: Absolute-Value Squeeze Theorem
gloss: This is the estimate form of the squeeze theorem. Instead of constructing two explicit bounding sequences, it bounds the error $|x_n-L|$ by a null sequence.

title: Pointwise Sum of Sequences
gloss: The sum sequence is formed term by term. No limiting process is involved in the definition; convergence enters only when the behavior of the resulting sequence is studied.

title: Pointwise Difference of Sequences
gloss: The difference sequence subtracts corresponding terms. It is equivalently the sum of $(x_n)$ with the pointwise negation of $(y_n)$.

title: Scalar Multiple of a Sequence
gloss: Scalar multiplication rescales every term by the same real number. It changes the vertical scale of the sequence but not its indexing.

title: Pointwise Negation of a Sequence
gloss: Pointwise negation reflects the sequence across zero. It is the special scalar multiple corresponding to the scalar $-1$.

title: Pointwise Product of Sequences
gloss: The product sequence multiplies corresponding terms. The product law for limits is subtler than the sum law because one factor must be controlled while the other is approximated.

title: Reciprocal Sequence
gloss: The reciprocal sequence is defined only when division by each term is legal. For limit theorems, a nonzero limit guarantees eventual nonzero behavior, but the sequence-level construction itself requir

title: Pointwise Quotient of Sequences
gloss: The quotient sequence is the product of $(x_n)$ with the reciprocal of $(y_n)$. Its convergence law is therefore a product law plus a reciprocal law.

title: Square Sequence
gloss: The square sequence is the product of a sequence with itself. Its limit law is therefore a direct specialization of the product law.

title: Absolute Value Sequence
gloss: The absolute value sequence records the magnitude of each term. It removes sign information while preserving distance from zero.

title: Square Root Sequence
gloss: The square root sequence is defined term by term on the nonnegative real terms. The nonnegativity hypothesis is part of the construction, not a proof artifact.

title: Limit of a Scalar Multiple
gloss: Multiplying every term by a fixed scalar multiplies the limiting value by the same scalar. The case $\alpha=0$ collapses the sequence to the constant zero sequence.

title: Limit of a Sum
gloss: The sum law says that convergent sequences may be added before or after taking limits. The proof splits a target tolerance into two smaller tolerances, one for each summand.

title: Limit of a Negation
gloss: Negation is scalar multiplication by $-1$, so this theorem is the scalar multiple law in its most common special case.

title: Limit of a Difference
gloss: The difference law follows by adding $(x_n)$ to the negation of $(y_n)$. It is therefore not a new analytic mechanism, but a useful named rule.

title: Limit of a Product
gloss: The product law uses both approximation and boundedness. A convergent sequence is eventually bounded, so one factor can be controlled while the other is made small.

title: Nonzero Limit is Eventually Nonzero
gloss: A sequence converging to a nonzero number eventually stays away from zero. This is the denominator-control result needed for reciprocal and quotient rules.

title: Limit of a Reciprocal
gloss: Reciprocation is continuous away from zero. The nonzero limit supplies a tail on which the denominators stay uniformly separated from zero.

title: Limit of a Quotient
gloss: The quotient law is the product law applied to $(x_n)$ and the reciprocal of $(y_n)$. The condition $M\neq 0$ prevents the limiting denominator from collapsing.

title: Limit of a Square
gloss: Squaring preserves limits because it is multiplication of a sequence by itself.

title: Limit of an Absolute Value
gloss: Taking absolute values cannot increase distance by more than the original distance: the magnitude function is continuous on $\mathbb{R}$.

title: Positive Limit is Eventually Positive
gloss: Convergence to a positive limit eventually traps the sequence inside the positive half-line. This result is the sign-control counterpart of the eventual nonzero theorem.

title: Limit of a Square Root
gloss: The square-root function is continuous on the nonnegative real line. Near zero the proof uses the order relation; away from zero it can be rationalized by multiplying by the conjugate.

title: Polynomial Sequence Limit
gloss: Polynomial limits are built from finitely many additions, scalar multiples, and products. The theorem packages those algebra laws into one reusable composition principle.

title: Rational Sequence Limit
