title: $M$-Tail of a Sequence
gloss: The $M$-tail discards the first $M - 1$ terms and retains everything from index $M$ onward. It is the formal object underlying the phrase \emph{all but finitely many terms}: to say that all but finite

title: Convergence of a Tail
gloss: Convergence is a \emph{tail property}: it depends only on the eventual behavior of the sequence, not on any finite initial segment. Discarding the first $m$ terms neither creates nor destroys converge

title: Convergence by Domination
gloss: The theorem is a \emph{domination principle}: it is not necessary to verify the $\varepsilon$-$N$ condition directly for $(x_n)$. It suffices to find a null sequence $(a_n)$ -- one converging to zero

title: Ratio Limit Less Than One Implies Null
gloss: This is the sequence form of the ratio test. The theorem does not merely say that the terms decrease eventually; it says they decrease at an eventually geometric rate. The strict inequality $L<1$ is e

title: Increasing Sequence
gloss: An increasing sequence may stay flat. The term ``increasing'' is therefore used here in the non-strict sense: each term is at least as large as the one before it.

title: Decreasing Sequence
gloss: A decreasing sequence may stay flat. The order condition says the sequence never rises as the index advances.

title: Strictly Increasing Sequence
gloss: Strict increase excludes equality between consecutive terms. Every strictly increasing sequence is increasing, but the converse need not hold.

title: Strictly Decreasing Sequence
gloss: Strict decrease excludes equality between consecutive terms. Every strictly decreasing sequence is decreasing, but the converse need not hold.

title: Monotone Sequence
gloss: Monotonicity means the sequence has one global direction. It may move upward, move downward, or stay constant, but it cannot reverse direction.

title: Eventually Increasing Sequence
gloss: Eventual increase allows finitely many early reversals. Only the tail of the sequence must move in a nondecreasing direction.

title: Eventually Decreasing Sequence
gloss: Eventual decrease allows the initial terms to behave irregularly. The order condition begins only after some finite threshold.

title: Eventually Monotone Sequence
gloss: Eventual monotonicity is the tail version of monotonicity. It is often the right hypothesis in applications, because finitely many early terms do not affect convergence.

title: Monotone Convergence Theorem
gloss: Boundedness prevents escape, and monotonicity prevents oscillation. Together they force convergence. The theorem is one of the standard forms in which the completeness of the real numbers becomes a se

title: Strict Monotonicity Implies Monotonicity
gloss: Strict monotonicity is stronger than non-strict monotonicity because every strict inequality is also a weak inequality.

title: Bounded Monotone Sequence Equivalences
gloss: For monotone sequences, the only obstruction to real convergence is escape in the monotone direction. Boundedness removes that obstruction.

title: Eventually Monotone Convergence Theorem
gloss: This is the tail version of the Monotone Convergence Theorem. Once the tail is monotone and bounded in the right direction, finite initial behavior is irrelevant.

title: Unbounded Monotone Divergence
gloss: For a monotone sequence, unboundedness has a direction. An increasing sequence that has no upper bound eventually exceeds every real number; the decreasing case is the order-dual statement.

title: Algebraic Transformations Preserve Monotonicity
gloss: Translation preserves order, multiplication by a positive scalar preserves order, and multiplication by a negative scalar reverses order. Negation is the special case $\alpha=-1$.

title: Strictly Increasing Index Map
gloss: The index map selects terms without repetition and without changing their order. This is the structural difference between a subsequence and an arbitrary rearrangement.

title: Subsequence
gloss: A subsequence keeps the original chronological order but may skip terms. The new index $k$ counts the selected terms, while $\sigma(k)$ records where each selected term came from in the original seque

title: Subsequential Limit
gloss: Subsequential limits detect limiting behavior along an infinite selection of terms. A sequence may have no ordinary limit but still have one or more subsequential limits.

title: Has Convergent Subsequence
gloss: This property asks for at least one convergent infinite selection. It does not assert convergence of the whole sequence and does not require uniqueness of the subsequential limit.

title: Subsequence Indices Dominate the Identity
gloss: A subsequence cannot outrun the original indexing backwards. By the time the subsequence has selected its $k$th term, it must have reached at least the $k$th original position.

title: Subsequences Preserve Limits
gloss: Subsequences cannot escape a limit already possessed by the whole sequence. The domination of subsequence indices by the identity transfers every tail estimate for $(x_n)$ to a tail estimate for $(x_{

title: Subsequential Limit of a Convergent Sequence
gloss: A convergent sequence has no hidden alternative limiting behavior. Every convergent subsequence inherits the same limit, and uniqueness of limits then forces equality.

title: Divergence by Two Subsequential Limits
gloss: Two different subsequential limits reveal persistent oscillation or splitting. If the whole sequence had a limit, every subsequence would have to inherit that same limit.

title: Boundedness Passes to Subsequences
gloss: A subsequence cannot create new values; it only selects from the original sequence. Any global bound for the original sequence is automatically a bound for the selection.

title: Monotonicity Passes to Subsequences
gloss: Selecting terms in the original order preserves the order pattern already present in the sequence. This is why the index map must be strictly increasing.

title: Subsequence of a Subsequence
gloss: Passing to subsequences is closed under iteration. The composed index map $\sigma\circ\tau$ is still strictly increasing.

title: Eventual Properties Pass to Subsequences
gloss: Subsequences eventually enter every tail of the original sequence. Any property that holds on a tail of the original therefore holds on a tail of the subsequence.

title: Frequent Properties Yield Subsequences
gloss: An infinitely recurring property can be threaded into a subsequence. The construction chooses one later witness at each step.

title: Subsequential Limits Respect Bounds
gloss: Bounds pass to subsequences, and limits preserve eventual inequalities. Hence subsequential limits remain inside any closed interval that contains all terms.

title: Squeeze Passes to Subsequences
gloss: Squeeze hypotheses are tail hypotheses, so they survive passage to subsequences.

title: Monotone Subsequence Theorem
gloss: Every sequence contains an infinite ordered pattern. The proof uses the peak dichotomy: either there are infinitely many terms larger than all later terms, or there is an infinite rising chain.

title: Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem for Sequences
gloss: Bolzano-Weierstrass is the compactness principle for bounded real sequences. Boundedness prevents escape, and the monotone subsequence theorem supplies an ordered subsequence to which the Monotone Con

title: Sequential Compactness of Closed Bounded Intervals
gloss: Closed bounded intervals are sequentially compact: a sequence trapped in the interval has a convergent subsequence, and closedness keeps the subsequential limit inside the interval.

title: Divergent Sequence
gloss: Divergence is the formal negation of real convergence. It says that every candidate limit fails at some fixed tolerance on every tail.

title: Divergence to Positive Infinity
gloss: Divergence to $+\infty$ means every horizontal threshold is eventually left below the sequence. It is not convergence to a real number; it is escape from the real line in the positive direction.

title: Divergence to Negative Infinity
gloss: Divergence to $-\infty$ is escape past every lower threshold. It is the order-dual of divergence to $+\infty$.

title: Oscillatory Sequence
gloss: Oscillation means failure to settle without one-directional escape. The sequence may remain bounded, as $(-1)^n$ does, or it may be unbounded while still repeatedly returning in incompatible direction

title: Divergence to Infinity Implies Real Divergence
gloss: Infinity divergence is a stronger, directional failure of real convergence. A sequence escaping past every real threshold cannot approach a finite real limit.

title: Two Subsequential Limits Force Divergence
gloss: Convergence forces all subsequences to inherit the same limit. Two different subsequential limits therefore witness an irreparable conflict in the tail behavior of the original sequence.

title: Unbounded Above Sequence Has a Positive-Infinity Subsequence
gloss: Unboundedness above may be sparse. This theorem says the high values can still be selected in increasing index order to form a subsequence that escapes to $+\infty$.

title: Unbounded Below Sequence Has a Negative-Infinity Subsequence
gloss: Unboundedness below can be converted into a selected tail that escapes past every lower threshold. The proof is the order-reversed version of the positive-infinity subsequence construction.

title: Bounded Divergence Produces Two Subsequential Limits
gloss: Bounded divergence cannot be explained by escape to infinity. In the real line, compactness forces convergent subsequences, and divergence forces at least two incompatible subsequential limits.

title: Tail Supremum Sequence
gloss: The term $s_n$ records the least ceiling of the $n$-th tail. As $n$ grows, earlier terms are discarded, so the tail has fewer opportunities to reach large values.

title: Tail Infimum Sequence
gloss: The term $i_n$ records the greatest floor of the $n$-th tail. As earlier terms are discarded, the tail loses opportunities to reach small values.

title: Limit Superior of a Sequence
gloss: The limit superior is the eventual upper envelope of the sequence. It is the number to which the ceilings of the tails descend.

title: Limit Inferior of a Sequence
gloss: The limit inferior is the eventual lower envelope of the sequence. It is the number to which the floors of the tails ascend.

title: Tail Suprema Are Decreasing
gloss: The $(n+1)$-st tail is contained in the $n$-th tail. Passing from a set to a smaller subset cannot raise its supremum.

title: Tail Infima Are Increasing
gloss: The $(n+1)$-st tail is contained in the $n$-th tail. Passing from a set to a smaller subset cannot lower its infimum.

title: Liminf Is Below Limsup
gloss: Every tail infimum lies below every corresponding tail supremum. Passing to the limits preserves this order.

title: Convergence iff Liminf Equals Limsup
gloss: Convergence occurs exactly when the eventual lower envelope and eventual upper envelope collapse to the same number.

title: Limsup Is the Largest Subsequential Limit
gloss: The limit superior is not merely an upper bound on subsequential behavior. It is actually attained as a subsequential limit, and no subsequential limit lies above it.

title: Liminf Is the Smallest Subsequential Limit
gloss: The limit inferior is attained as a subsequential limit and no subsequential limit lies below it.

title: Oscillation Criterion via Liminf and Limsup
gloss: A strict gap between the lower and upper envelopes is exactly persistent oscillation in bounded sequences. The sequence keeps returning near at least two incompatible limiting levels.

title: Limsup Comparison Under Eventual Order
gloss: Eventual order of sequences passes to eventual order of their upper tail envelopes. Taking the limiting ceiling preserves that comparison.

title: Liminf Comparison Under Eventual Order
gloss: Eventual order also passes to the lower tail envelopes. If one sequence is eventually below another, its eventual floor cannot sit above the other's eventual floor.

title: Limsup Squeeze Under Eventual Order
gloss: If one sequence is eventually trapped between two others, then its eventual upper envelope is trapped between their eventual upper envelopes.

title: Liminf Squeeze Under Eventual Order
gloss: The lower-envelope version of the squeeze principle says that eventual two-sided order traps the eventual floors as well as the eventual ceilings.

title: Increasing Sequence Limit as Supremum
gloss: The supremum is the only possible destination: all terms lie below it, and the defining approximation property of the supremum forces the sequence eventually above every lower tolerance.

title: Decreasing Sequence Limit as Infimum
gloss: The infimum is the decreasing analogue of the supremum target: the sequence falls toward its greatest lower barrier.

title: Tail Suprema and Infima Converge
gloss: Tail suprema decrease because later tails contain fewer terms; tail infima increase for the same reason. Boundedness supplies the opposite bound needed for monotone convergence.

title: Bounded Sequence Has Limsup and Liminf
gloss: For bounded sequences, limsup and liminf are not extra assumptions. They are guaranteed order-theoretic limits of the tail envelopes.

title: Cluster Value of a Sequence
gloss: No matter how far out in the sequence one looks, some later term returns inside every neighborhood of $L$.

title: Cluster Values Are Subsequential Limits
gloss: The neighborhood formulation and the subsequence formulation describe the same phenomenon: infinitely many opportunities to get arbitrarily close to $L$.

title: Bounded Sequences Have Cluster Values
gloss: Boundedness traps the sequence in a finite interval, and Bolzano-Weierstrass extracts a convergent subsequence from that trap.

title: Limsup and Liminf Are Extremal Cluster Values
gloss: Limsup and liminf describe the top and bottom edges of the sequence's subsequential behavior.

title: Cauchy Sequence
gloss: The Cauchy condition says that the tails of the sequence eventually have arbitrarily small internal spread. It does not name a candidate limit; it only asserts that late terms become mutually close.

title: Convergent Sequences Are Cauchy
gloss: Once every sufficiently late term is close to the same limit, any two sufficiently late terms are close to each other. This theorem is the easy half of the Cauchy criterion.

title: Cauchy Sequences Are Bounded
gloss: A Cauchy sequence has one tightly controlled tail. The finitely many terms before that tail can be absorbed into a single larger bound.

title: Cauchy Sequence with a Convergent Subsequence
gloss: The Cauchy condition prevents the rest of the tail from wandering away from any convergent subsequence. A single subsequential limit therefore determines the limit of the whole sequence.

title: Cauchy Criterion for Real Sequences
gloss: This theorem is the sequence form of completeness of $\mathbb{R}$. In an incomplete ambient space, a sequence may be internally stable without converging inside that space.

title: Cauchy Criterion via Tails
gloss: The Cauchy condition is a tail property. Reindexing a sufficiently late tail does not change the substance of the condition; it only changes the names of the indices.

title: Cauchy Tail Diameter Criterion
gloss: The tail-diameter form says that once one tail is small, every later tail is small as well. It records the monotone nature of tail control without introducing additional notation for diameters.

title: Cauchy Sequences Have Vanishing Successive Differences
gloss: Cauchy control applies to every pair of late indices, so it applies in particular to consecutive late indices. The converse is false in general: small successive steps do not prevent accumulated drift

title: Scalar Multiples of Cauchy Sequences
gloss: Multiplying all terms by one fixed real number rescales all pairwise tail distances by the same fixed factor.

title: Sums of Cauchy Sequences
gloss: The triangle inequality splits the tail distance of the sum into the two tail distances already controlled by the original sequences.

title: Differences of Cauchy Sequences
gloss: The difference theorem is the sum theorem applied after negating one sequence. It is often the form used to compare two approximating sequences.

title: Linear Combinations of Cauchy Sequences
gloss: The Cauchy real sequences are closed under finite real linear combinations. This is the additive vector-space part of Cauchy algebra.

title: Products of Cauchy Sequences
gloss: Products require boundedness in addition to tail closeness. The boundedness is supplied automatically because every Cauchy real sequence is bounded.

title: Reciprocals of Cauchy Sequences
gloss: The lower bound away from zero prevents division from amplifying small tail differences without control. Without it, reciprocals need not preserve Cauchy behavior.

title: Quotients of Cauchy Sequences
gloss: The quotient theorem is the product theorem combined with the reciprocal theorem for a denominator eventually bounded away from zero.

title: Absolute Values of Cauchy Sequences
gloss: The reverse triangle inequality bounds the distance between absolute values by the original distance between terms.
